papers.title: Formation and spatial distribution of hypervelocity stars in AGN outflows

abstract.text: We study star formation within outflows driven by active galactic nuclei (AGN) as a new source of hypervelocity stars (HVSs). Recent observations revealed active star formation inside a galactic outflow at a rate of $\\\\sim 15\\\\,M_{\\\\odot}\\\\,\\\\rm yr^{-1}$. We verify that the shells swept up by an AGN outflow are capable of cooling and fragmentation into cold clumps embedded in a hot tenuous gas via thermal instabilities. We show that cold clumps of $\\\\sim 10^3\\\\,M_{\\\\odot}$ are formed within $\\\\sim 10^5$ yrs. As a result, stars are produced along outflow's path, endowed with the outflow speed at their formation site. These HVSs travel through the galactic halo and eventually escape into the intergalactic medium. The expected instantaneous rate of star formation inside the outflow is $\\\\sim 4-5$ orders of magnitude greater than the average rate associated with previously proposed mechanisms for producing HVSs, such as the Hills mechanism and three-body interaction between a star and a black hole binary. We predict the spatial distribution of HVSs formed in AGN outflows for future observational probe.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper presents a novel mechanism for hypervelocity star (HVS) formation in active galactic nucleus (AGN) outflows, where thermal instabilities drive cooling and fragmentation of outflowing gas into cold clumps of ~10³ M⊙ within ~10⁵ years. These clumps form with the outflow’s velocity, resulting in stars born with high speeds that escape into the intergalactic medium, producing HVSs at a rate ~10⁴–10⁵ times greater than the Hills mechanism. The spatial distribution of such HVSs is predicted to exhibit a distinct longitudinal clumpiness, offering a new observational signature for future surveys.

source_fields.title: Formation and spatial distribution of hypervelocity stars in AGN outflows

source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper proposes a novel mechanism for hypervelocity star formation in AGN outflows via fragmentation of cold molecular clumps formed through thermal instability, offering an alternative and significantly more abundant production channel—consistent with observed HVS rates and predicting a distinct spatial distribution not previously considered in the literature. Two-phase medium: AGN outflows exhibit a transition from momentum-driven to energy-driven phases, where Rayleigh-Taylor instabilities fragment the wind into transient clumps, and subsequent cooling enables the formation of stable, cold clumps via thermal instability in the energy-conserving regime. Hypervelocity stars: Hypervelocity stars are formed in AGN outflows through the rapid gravitational collapse of turbulent clumps within swept-up material, with their velocity distribution matching the outflow and a significant fraction exceeding hundreds of km/s at the halo boundary, making them potential hypervelocity stars. Summary \\& Discussion: This study finds that AGN outflows can produce high-velocity stars (≥500 km s⁻¹) at a rate of 1–10 M⊙ yr⁻¹ over 50–100 kpc, with speeds matching the Milky Way's HVS population and a production rate 4–5 orders of magnitude higher than other mechanisms, due to thermal instability-driven star formation in shocked gas.

abstract: We study star formation within outflows driven by active galactic nuclei (AGN) as a new source of hypervelocity stars (HVSs).
As a result, stars are produced along outflow's path, endowed with the outflow speed at their formation site.
title: Formation and spatial distribution of hypervelocity stars in AGN outflows

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical paper studying formation mechanism and spatial distribution predictions without presenting observational data
